title: Peppermint tea for indigestion
subtitle: The kitchen's after-dinner cup
excerpt: A simple hot infusion of dried peppermint leaves. Traditionally taken after a heavy meal for indigestion, mild bloating, or trapped wind.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), peppermint entry: the traditional after-meal cup, the carminative action, the volatile-oil discussion. EMA HMPC monograph on Mentha × piperita folium (2008): the conservative modern dosing reference (1.5-3 g dried leaf per cup, up to three times a day).

content.text: One cup of peppermint tea, taken after a heavy meal. The kitchen's after-dinner cup for indigestion, mild bloating, and trapped wind. Two teaspoons of dried peppermint leaves in a cup. Just-off-boil water poured over. The lid kept on while it steeps. Ten minutes from kettle to first sip.
Peppermint sits in the
carminative

family of herbs, fennel, chamomile, dill, caraway. Carminatives are traditionally taken at the end of a meal to ease the wind and spasm that come from over-eating, eating too fast, or sitting down to a meal already wound up. The action is the volatile oils, menthol, menthone, which the lid traps in the cup so they reach the drinker, not the ceiling.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Reflux: peppermint can relax the lower oesophageal sphincter and worsen heartburn in readers with significant gastro-oesophageal reflux. If a cup makes your reflux worse rather than better, switch to chamomile or fennel.

Children: peppermint oil should not be applied to the face or chest of infants and small children (apnea risk from menthol vapours). The dilute tea at standard kitchen strength is tolerated from school age upward. For children under five, use a teaspoon of dried herb per cup at most, and skip the tea entirely if a child has a cough or breathing condition.

See your doctor if your indigestion wakes you at night, comes with unexplained weight loss, painful swallowing, vomiting, black or tarry stools, or chest pain that radiates to the arm or jaw.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

content.text: What you need

items.name: Dried peppermint leaves
items.prepNote: about 2 teaspoons of loose-leaf dried peppermint

content.text: The 1 part herb to 100 parts water ratio (1 g dried leaf per 100 ml water, doubled for a strong cup) is the standard infusion ratio. Use loose-leaf dried peppermint, supermarket tea bags work but most are cut so finely that the volatile oils are half-evaporated by the time you open the box. A herbalist supplier or a health-food shop will sell whole-leaf dried peppermint that smells strongly of mint when the bag opens.
Method
Boil the water, then wait

Boil the kettle. As it boils, scoop {{dried-peppermint-leaves}} of dried peppermint leaves into a teapot or a tea infuser in a cup. When the kettle clicks off, count to thirty before pouring, water above ~95°C cooks the volatile oils off and the cup goes flat. The brief wait drops it into the right band.
Steep covered, 8-10 minutes

Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over the leaves. Put the lid on the teapot, or sit a saucer over the cup. The lid is the rule, without it the volatile oils drift out as steam, and what's left in the cup is half the remedy. Set a timer for 8-10 minutes; longer doesn't deepen the action and starts to drag out the tannins, which can make a strong
infusion
taste astringent at the back of the throat.
Strain and sip

Strain through the infuser or a fine sieve into the cup. Honey is optional and best added once the tea has cooled below 60°C, hotter and the honey loses its raw character and becomes just sweet liquid. Drink while still warm, slowly, over about ten minutes. The action begins in the first sip; finishing the cup before the food has had time to move on gives the herbs the most useful window.
Dosing

One cup after the meal that is causing the trouble. Up to three cups a day during a flare-up: morning, mid-afternoon, and after the evening meal. The traditional course is short, a few cups across a day or two until things settle. If your indigestion is daily, the home remedy is not the right tool. Book in with your GP. Strong daily peppermint over weeks can also deepen reflux in some people, and the daily cup can hide the question your doctor needs to ask.

Paediatric use is not adult-divided-by-half. Consult a herbalist who specialises in children's herbal medicine, or a paediatric doctor, before using peppermint preparations in children under twelve. For school-aged children at half-strength (1 g per 200 ml, steeped 5 minutes), one cup after a meal is the traditional limit.
Storage

Dried peppermint leaves keep 12 months in a tin or dark glass jar with a tight lid, in a cool dark cupboard away from the cooker. The herb smells strongly of mint when the lid comes off; if the smell has gone faint, the volatile oils have gone with it and the cup will be weak, top up the stock. Brewed tea doesn't keep, make each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for indigestion that wakes you at night. Also for any of these: weight loss you can't explain, painful swallowing, vomiting, black or tarry stools, or chest pain that runs into the arm or jaw. Daily bloating for three weeks or more needs a check too. So does bloating with weight loss or a change in bowel habit. Home tea is not the right tool for any of those.

Skip peppermint tea if you have significant gastro-oesophageal reflux, peppermint can make heartburn worse rather than better. Chamomile or fennel are the better carminatives in that case. Avoid in pregnancy at therapeutic dose if you have a history of miscarriage or early labour, the culinary cup is fine; the strong medicinal three-times-a-day cup is one to clear with your midwife first. Skip entirely in infants and very small children.

attrs.intro: The three usual reasons a peppermint cup falls flat.

items.fix: Wait 30 seconds after the kettle clicks off before pouring. Put the lid on. If a fresh bag of leaves still produces a faint cup, your supplier is selling old stock, try a herbalist-grade source.
items.cause: Water was too hot, or the lid was off, or the dried herb is past its smell-strong window.
items.symptom: Cup tastes thin and barely minty

items.fix: Switch to chamomile or fennel. Both are carminatives without the sphincter-relaxing effect.
items.cause: Peppermint relaxes the valve at the top of the stomach. In strong reflux that lets acid up.
items.symptom: Cup makes the heartburn worse, not better

items.fix: Stop at 8-10 minutes. Set a timer; the leaves keep darkening the water past the useful point.
items.cause: Steeped too long, drawing out the tannins.
items.symptom: Tea is sharp and astringent at the back of the throat

attrs.heading: If the cup didn't work
